---
title: How Many More Points?
author: Walter Dal'Maz Silva
date: today
execute:
  python: .venv/Scripts/python.exe
  cache: true
  echo: false
format:
  typst:
    logo: _assets/logo.svg
    fig-format: png
    fig-dpi: 300
jupyter:
  jupytext:
    text_representation:
      extension: .qmd
      format_name: quarto
      format_version: '1.0'
      jupytext_version: 1.19.4
  kernelspec:
    display_name: Python 3 (ipykernel)
    language: python
    name: python3
---


In this note we will *empirically* investigate the convergence of a data acquisition stream simulated by a simple white noise. The goal is to create an intuition regarding the stabilization of the the streamed data to *know* when to pause an experiment. Here we start by importing the standard tools we will use in this project.

```{python}
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

from typing import Generator, Tuple, Union
```

## The algorithm

An stable estimation of the mean $\mu_n$ of a streaming sample of data points $x_k$ can be done with help of [Welford's algorigthm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algorithms_for_calculating_variance). The updated mean $\mu_n$ after $n$ data points have been streamed (acquired) can be updated from the previous value $\mu_{n-1}$ and the new data point $x_n$ as: 

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= x_n - \mu_{n-1}
\\[12pt]
\mu_n &= \mu_{n-1} + \frac{\Delta}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

The quantity $\Delta$ has been introduced above as it will also be used to update the running sum of squares $M_{2,n}$ as:

$$
M_{2,n} = M_{2,n-1} + \Delta \cdotp (x_n - \mu_n)
$$.

The sample standard deviation (using Bessel's correction $n-1$) is computed as

$$
s_n = \sqrt{\frac{M_{2,n}}{n - 1}}
$$

and the population standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_n = \sqrt{\frac{M_{2,n}}{n}}
$$

{{< pagebreak >}}

The class `StreamingCalculator` provides an implementation of this algorithm.

```{python}
#| echo: true
class StreamingCalculator:
    """ Welford generator for mean and sample standard deviation."""
    
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._generator = self._update()
        next(self._generator)

    def _update(self) -> Generator[Tuple[float, float], float, None]:
        """ The underlying generator handling the recursive Welford logic. """
        n: int = 0
        mean: float = 0.0
        M2: float = 0.0
        
        val = yield (mean, 0.0)
        
        while val is not None:
            n += 1
            delta = val - mean
            mean += delta / n
            M2 += delta * (val - mean)

            std = (M2 / (n - 1)) ** 0.5 if n > 1 else 0.0
            val = yield (mean, std)

    def __call__(self, val: float) -> Tuple[float, float]:
        """ Pass a new value by calling the as a function object."""
        return self._generator.send(val)
```

## Application

```{python}
#| echo: true
m_true = 5.0
s_true = 5.0
r_true = np.log10(m_true / s_true)

rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=43)
data = rng.normal(loc=m_true, scale=s_true, size=100_000)

m_eval = np.mean(data)
s_eval = np.std(data)
```

```{python}
#| echo: true
stream_stats = StreamingCalculator()

table = np.array(list(map(stream_stats, data)))
m = table[:, 0]
s = table[:, 1]

epsilon = 1.0e-10
r = np.log10(m / (s + epsilon))
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: Analysis of convergence
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: 80%

plt.close("all")
plt.style.use("classic")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(
    nrows     = 3, 
    ncols     = 2, 
    figsize   = (8, 8), 
    facecolor = "white",
    sharex    = True,
    height_ratios = [0.8, 0.9, 0.8]
)
ax = np.ravel(ax.T)

ax[0].semilogx(m, c="k")
ax[1].semilogx(s, c="k")
ax[2].semilogx(r, c="k")

ax[3].semilogx(np.log10(np.abs(np.diff(m))), c="k")
ax[4].semilogx(np.log10(np.abs(np.diff(s))), c="k")
ax[5].semilogx(np.log10(np.abs(np.diff(r))), c="k")

ax[0].axhline(m_true, color="r", linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
ax[1].axhline(s_true, color="r", linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
ax[2].axhline(r_true, color="r", linestyle="--", linewidth=2)

ax[3].axhline(0.0, color="r", linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
ax[4].axhline(0.0, color="r", linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
ax[5].axhline(0.0, color="r", linestyle="--", linewidth=2)

ax[0].grid(linestyle=":", linewidth=0.5)
ax[1].grid(linestyle=":", linewidth=0.5)
ax[2].grid(linestyle=":", linewidth=0.5)
ax[3].grid(linestyle=":", linewidth=0.5)
ax[4].grid(linestyle=":", linewidth=0.5)
ax[5].grid(linestyle=":", linewidth=0.5)

ax[0].set_ylim(4.6, 5.4)
ax[1].set_ylim(4.6, 5.4)
ax[2].set_ylim(-0.1, 0.1)
# ax[3].set_ylim(-0.01, 0.1)
# ax[4].set_ylim(-0.01, 0.1)
# ax[5].set_ylim(-0.01, 0.1)

fig.suptitle((f"Population $\\mathcal{{N}}({m_true:.0f}; {s_true:.0f})$ | "
              f"Sample $\\mathcal{{N}}({m_eval:.0f}; {s_eval:.0f})$ "), 
             fontsize = "x-large", fontweight = "normal")

fig.tight_layout()
```
